title: What to Do If Your Matric Results Are Not What You Expected
author: Tklesson Team
content:
You open your National Senior Certificate results and the marks are not what you expected. Maybe your APS is lower than you needed for your chosen course. Maybe you passed but did not achieve the level required in Mathematics or Physical Sciences. Maybe you did not pass at all. The disappointment is real and deserves to be felt. But a disappointing matric result in South Africa is not the end of your education. It is a detour, not a dead end. Here are your options and how to choose the right path forward.

Allow Yourself to Feel It, Then Plan

A disappointing examination result is a loss, and loss deserves acknowledgment. Give yourself a few days to process the disappointment. Talk to people who care about you. Do not make major decisions while you are still in the emotional aftermath of the news. After a few days, shift from feeling to planning. The question is no longer why did this happen. It is what do I do now. The learners who recover from academic disappointment are not the ones who never experienced it. They are the ones who moved from emotional reaction to strategic action as quickly as possible.

Option One: Apply for Remarking or a Supplementary Examination

If your results are close to the next achievement level or if you believe an error may have occurred in the marking, you can apply for a remark. The Department of Basic Education allows learners to request that specific subjects be remarked. There is a fee per subject, which is refunded if your mark changes. The deadline for remark applications is typically within a few weeks of the results being released. Apply promptly if you intend to request a remark. The forms are available through your school or the Department of Basic Education website.

If you failed a subject or did not achieve the level you needed, you may qualify for a supplementary examination. Supplementary exams are typically written in February or March of the following year. They are available to learners who did not pass the NSC, who did not meet the requirements for a specific course, or who were unable to write the examination due to illness or other valid reasons. Check the specific eligibility criteria on the Department of Basic Education website. The supplementary examination gives you a second chance without waiting a full year.

Option Two: Adjust Your Course or Institution Choices

If your APS meets the requirements for university admission but is below the competitive threshold for your intended course, consider adjusting your course choice. A learner who wanted Medicine but did not achieve the required APS and subject levels may qualify for Nursing, Pharmacy, or a Bachelor of Health Sciences. These are meaningful careers in the healthcare sector, and some can serve as a foundation for postgraduate study in Medicine later. A learner who wanted Engineering may qualify for a related technical field or a diploma program that leads to a degree through a progression pathway.

Consider applying to a different institution. The competitive thresholds at top universities like the University of Cape Town or the University of the Witwatersrand are higher than at many other South African universities that offer equally accredited programs. A course that is out of reach at one institution may be accessible at another. The name of the university matters less than the quality of the education and the opportunities it provides. Many successful South African professionals graduated from institutions that were not their first choice.

Option Three: Pursue a Diploma or Higher Certificate Pathway

If your results do not qualify you for direct entry into a Bachelor's degree program, diploma and higher certificate programs provide alternative routes. Technical and vocational education and training colleges across South Africa offer programs in engineering, information technology, business, hospitality, and health sciences. These programs are typically shorter than degrees, more practically focused, and lead directly to employment or to further study. A learner who completes a diploma can often articulate into a degree program at a university of technology or a comprehensive university, entering at an advanced level.

Universities of technology, such as the Cape Peninsula University of Technology, the Durban University of Technology, and the Tshwane University of Technology, offer career-focused qualifications with lower APS requirements than traditional universities. The education is practical and industry-relevant. Graduates are often highly employable because they have developed skills that employers need. The diploma route takes longer than direct degree entry, but it arrives at a valuable qualification.

Option Four: Explore Bridging Courses and Extended Degree Programs

Many South African universities offer extended degree programs that provide an additional year of foundational study before the standard degree curriculum begins. These programs are designed for learners who show potential but whose matric results do not meet the standard entry requirements. The extended program provides additional support in the early years to help you succeed. Upon completion, you earn the same degree as students who entered through the standard route. The extended program takes an extra year, but that year is an investment in your success.

Bridging courses are shorter, typically a semester or a year, and focus on bringing your knowledge in a specific subject up to the required level. If your Mathematics mark is slightly below the requirement for your chosen course, a bridging course in Mathematics may close the gap. These courses are offered by some universities and by private institutions. Check with the universities you are interested in to see what bridging options they offer or recognize.

Option Five: Retake the NSC

If you are determined to pursue a specific course that your current results do not support, and you have exhausted the other options, you can retake the NSC as a part-time or full-time candidate. You do not need to retake every subject. You can register only for the subjects where you need improvement. The retake requires a year of preparation and the discipline to study independently or through a tutoring program. The learners who succeed in a retake are those who identify what went wrong in their first attempt and address it honestly.

Before committing to a retake, consider whether the course you are targeting is genuinely the only path to the career you want. Some careers have multiple entry routes. Some learners retake the NSC only to discover later that they could have reached the same destination through a diploma or bridging program with less time and cost. Research your options before deciding.

The Bottom Line

A disappointing NSC result in South Africa is not the end of your education. Apply for a remark or supplementary examination if you are close to the required threshold. Adjust your course or institution choices to match your results. Pursue a diploma, higher certificate, bridging course, or extended degree program. Retake the NSC if a specific course requires it and no alternative path exists. The learners who succeed after a disappointing matric result are those who refuse to let a single examination define their future. Your NSC marks are one measure of your performance on one set of tests. They are not a measure of your intelligence, your potential, or your worth.
